prose: The frigid, oil-slicked bite of the synth-alloy handrail dug into {given_name}'s calloused palm, a sudden, teeth-chattering frost that anchored them against the nauseating, heavy sway of the orbital station's slow rotation. Beneath the thin soles of their canvas boots, the deck plates did not merely hum; they bucked with the raw, rhythmic shudder of life-support pistons laboring against the absolute zero of the void—a mechanical tremor that rattled up their shins to nest in the hollow of their throat. This was the unvarnished marrow of the {family_name} rise: not some clean, imperial spire of polished quartz, but a patched, sweating cylinder of scrap-iron and copper rivets, keeping the crushing dark from the lungs of two hundred thousand laboring kin who had crossed an ocean of stars only to find the same cold dirt, merely suspended.

Every drag of the limbs in this low-gravity draft felt like wading through the yellow silt of a flooded riverbed, a constant, muscle-straining resistance against the urge to simply float away and dissolve into the dark. {given_name} dragged a thumb over the pitted, gritty casing of the standard-issue datapad, feeling the prickle of recycled carbon dust that settled over every surface like the coal-soot of their ancestors' old railway camps. The screen buzzed against their fingertips, a dry, stuttering haptic click that translated shipping manifests and border skirmishes into tiny, rhythmic vibrations—the only physical thread connecting their family's fragile foothold here to the vast, indifferent frontier where the {surname} name was either cursed as a threat or ignored as chattel.

The air itself was thick, almost tactile, a warm grease of ozone and scorched copper wire that clung to the skin like wet wool. {given_name}'s fingers, though spared the split-skin scars of the deep-bore asteroid miners, throbbed with the dull, aching stiffness born of decades in the cold and the white-knuckled grip required to hold a splintered faction together. To touch a console in these cramped, iron-ribbed quarters was not to command the heavens with a feather-light touch, but to throw one's entire body against a stubborn, rusted lever, feeling the bone-deep kickback of a history that had never wanted their kind to survive, let alone ascend.

The copper filaments of the projection grid stung your fingertips, static electricity snapping in tiny, needle-sharp sparks as your hand traced the icy, light-etched lines of the outer system shipping lanes.

choice.text: Strategize the next move to secure key trade advantages in the Outer Systems.

prose: Your bare palm pressed flat against the uninsulated bulkhead, feeling the shuddering, metallic heartbeat of the main scrubbers—a desperate, shaking vibration that was the only barrier between your people and the frozen, silent dark.

choice.text: Attend to the foundational stability and infrastructure of the current dominion.

decision.prompt: A small independent settlement on the newly terraformed planet Xylos-7 is requesting immediate aid, claiming their vital atmospheric processors are failing due to 'unforeseen seismic activity.' While humanitarian, providing aid would divert resources from your current, ambitious planetary survey project. How do you respond to their plea?

options.text: Dispatch a priority aid convoy immediately, prioritizing the lives of settlers above all else.
Send a rapid diagnostic team first to verify the claims; aid will follow only if truly critical.

prose: The high-vaulted chamber of the grand assembly did not speak in a single tongue, but in a layered, vibrating sediment of sound that rattled the bones of the ears like the gravel of the Sierra foothills tumbling in a sluice. Through the dampening fields, the dry, cured vowels of the Core's elder diplomats—reminiscent of cold Boston parlors—collided with the wet, percussive clatter of the outer-rim delegates, whose dialect had been hammered out in the communal dormitories of the western shipyards. Every dry cough, every dragging of carbon-mesh chairs against the cold flagstones, was absorbed and re-broadcast by the gallery's receiver-horns, a chaotic tide of noise that swelled to a roar as the high clerk unrolled the digital scroll for the Kepler-186f terraforming concession—an enterprise upon which the entire remaining fortune of the {family_name} name rested, balanced precariously like a gold piece on the rim of a wooden bucket.

Listening through his auditory implants, {given_name} tracked the real mechanics of power grinding beneath this parliamentary din. It was recorded in the asthmatic, soot-choked rattle of the old-world delegation's breath—lungs still scarred by the ancient coal-smoke of the early crossings—and in the sharp, rhythmic clicking of ivory abacuses beneath the mining cartel's table, a sober, orderly sound of the regional Huiguan councils that the western journals had so often slandered as lawless tongs. A rival delegate let out a long, wet hiss of air between his teeth, a sound like steam escaping a cold-iron radiator, dripping with the historic contempt of those who believed the western families were mere coolies masquerading as mandarins. To an ear trained by generations of survival between the mountain camps and the high-orbit dry docks, these micro-tonal shifts were the true ledger of the assembly, a map of alliances drawn in sighs, gasps, and the dry rustle of silk sleeves against cold steel desks.

Then came the signal: a clean, high-frequency chime that sliced through the chamber’s rumble with the cold edge of a butcher’s cleaver. It was the triple-tone priority chirp of the Oort-line relay, a distant and astronomically expensive signal that had traveled through light-hours of frozen void to reach the assembly's receiver, carrying the voices of the lonely, male-heavy pioneer outposts that had been starved of demographic balance for nearly a century before the late-era migrations unlocked the stars. The noise hung in the hot air, pure and silver, a stark reminder that while these gilded benches argued over old treaties and Pacific-rim precedents, the black frontier was screaming for steel, fuel, and the steady hand of families who knew how to build on stone. It was a sound that vibrated not in the ears, but in the marrow, calling the blood to look outward toward the vast, silent dark where empty worlds waited for a master's voice to command them.

The low, melodic baritone of {full_name} drifted across the floor, each syllable polished to a mirror shine, winding through the chamber to softly strip away the teeth of the {family_name} distribution bill under the guise of grandfathered precedent. It was a soft, sibilant sound, like silk sliding over a polished coffin.

choice.text: Engage the rival delegate, seeking a compromise that benefits the {family_name} line's long-term objectives.

prose: Through the thick, armored view-panes of the ceiling, the rhythmic, metallic hammer-blows of the orbital shipyard vibrated in the marrow—a dull, heavy thudding of iron on iron that spoke of new empires waiting to be welded together, echoing the ancient, steady strike of the sledgehammers that once conquered the granite of the Sierra Nevada.

choice.text: Direct attention to securing greater funding and resources for continued infrastructure expansion.

thread.crossing: The low-frequency vibration of {ireland} {given_name}’s sub-dermal vocalizer rattled through the shared brass balustrade, a secret, rhythmic cadence that only {chinese} {given_name}’s long-tuned auditory implants could isolate from the assembly's deafening roar. Across the tiered floor, the Irish delegate raised a glass of heavy synthetic spirit in a silent, challenging toast, sealing their unspoken pact to carve up the outer-rim shipping concessions before the Core diplomats could even vote. This quiet mutiny of two old diaspora lines, both forged in the hard labor of the Western railroads and the deep mines, bound their dynasties to a shared, highly profitable ascent, forever altering the balance of the high council.

prose: The double-paned quartz of the observation cupola bent the local binary glare into sharp, chromatic needles, splitting the distant, wild nebulae into bruised plums and chemical violet. Below, the curve of Proxima Centauri b hung like a cold, wet jade bead, its fledgling atmospheric dome a thin, pale turquoise skin humming with ionized silt. Through the polarizing filters, the first greening terrace-plots of this new world—cleared by the sweat and heavy capital of the {family_name} syndicate—showed as microscopic, emerald thread-strokes creeping over rust-red basalt, a frail, desperate handwriting of chlorophyll scratched against the crushing dark of the abyss.

Yet {given_name} looked past those fragile terraces toward the airless companion moon, where a jagged, basaltic tooth broke the horizon. The alien monolith did not reflect the light of the binary suns; it drank it whole, a clean, rectangular puncture in the starfields that warped the constellations behind it like a thick, hand-poured glass lens. It was a silent scar on the lunar crust, a monument of absolute stillness that made the busy, yellow welding torches of the orbital docks look small, frantic, and embarrassingly brief—a reminder that this vast frontier was already occupied by older, colder eyes before the first wooden junk ever put out from the shores of the old home.

Glaring, ancient light, cast from stars that had burned out before their family ever crossed the salt-plains, threw long, skeletal shadows across the copper-clad ribs of the station. This silent wash of starlight caught the gold-leaf trim of the cabin’s small family altar, illuminating the incense ash drifting in weightless circles between the safety of the bulkheads and the terrifying, unmeasured black. The path the {surname} line chose through this glittering, cold desert would either be swallowed by this ancient silence or write their name into the very bones of the stars, leaving a permanent mark where once there had been only an empty, unmonitored road.

Ahead lay the hot, glowing nurseries of the core, a vast, luminous pasture of raw hydrogen and stellar dust waiting to be corralled by the family’s heavy terraforming rigs, much like the wild valleys their grandfathers once tamed.

choice.text: Commit to rapid, aggressive colonization of all viable celestial bodies within their declared sector.

prose: The lunar monolith remained a cold, lightless wedge of absolute shadow, a silent question mark carved into the basalt that demanded a reckoning before any more seed-ships were launched into the dark.

choice.text: Prioritize scientific investigation and diplomatic outreach regarding the alien artifact, even if it delays expansion.

decision.prompt: The {family_name} line stands at a terrifying precipice: to lock down their hard-won holdings across the settled systems, or to risk everything on a desperate, grand leap into the unmapped deep. A proposed 'Galactic Sovereignty Pact' offers absolute security and dominion but fences in their ambition, while the rival 'Frontier Initiative' demands they pour their blood and capital into the outer dark with no promise of return. How will this generation carve the family's name into the stars?

options.text: Champion the Galactic Sovereignty Pact: Consolidate political control over established territories and build an unassailable interstellar empire.
Lead the Frontier Initiative: Invest heavily in deep-space exploration and colonization, pushing humanity's reach further into the unknown at all costs.
Propose a 'Balanced Development' plan: Prioritize sustainable growth and cultural exchange within existing colonies while funding cautious, ethical exploration.

prose: The first drop of the 'Star-Root' distillate is a brackish shock on {given_name}’s tongue, blooming into a musty, copper-heavy scald that tastes less of green loam than of the dead, pressurized cisterns below the ice-locks. It is a stubborn, unperfumed liquor, yet beneath the initial sting of nitrogen-starved fungus lingers a ghostly, fermented sweetness—the unmistakable echo of ancestral sorghum and scorched Yellow River clay, carried across six light-years of cold vacuum only to be drowned in industrial ethanol. This is the raw vintage of their labor, a draft brewed in the dark of the lower domes and offered up now to the successor as a salt-crust sacrament of what it costs to anchor a family to these barren rocks.

Around them, the remnants of the retirement feast grow dull under the flickering yellow lumens of the high table. Cold salt-pork, cured with synthetic saltpetre that leaves a metallic prickle at the back of the throat, sits in silver bowls beside soy-lacquered duck skin that dissolves into a greasy, engineered wax. The heavy tubers harvested from the deep-trench hydroponic bays taste faintly of plastic conduit and the gray, mineral silt of the water-reclaiming scrubbers. It is an abundance born entirely of filters and sealed valves, every mouthful weighted with the chalky grit of bone-density supplements and the stale, thrice-lunged chill of the station's recycled air.

As the final swallow burns down {given_name}’s throat, it leaves a lingering coat of bitter oil and cold iron that seems to settle in the very marrow. This is the true taste of sovereignty on the frontier: not the clean, rain-washed sweetness of a lost hemisphere, but the chemical grit of survival. To pass this cup to the heir is to pass a legacy of beautiful, necessary poisons, ensuring that the next {surname} to rule understands that power is never a clean draft, but something to be swallowed whole, tasted in the dark, and endured.

The tongue still recoils from the memory of the early days: the chalky, zinc-bitten gray paste of the first-generation ration-blocks, tasting of industrial yeast and dried silt—the dry, ash-like bread of the grandparents who broke their fingernails against the frozen iron.

choice.text: Reflect on the resilience forged by hardship, a vital lesson for the heir.

prose: Then comes the bright, shocking sting of a sun-grafted nectarine, its engineered pulp dripping with a syrupy, ozone-sharp sweetness that floods the mouth like a spring morning on a world that has never known a natural sky.

choice.text: Savor the achievements and new frontiers, instilling a sense of boundless potential in the heir.

decision.prompt: The bitter, chemical sting of the Star-Root still coats the tongue, a reminder that the {family_name} line has survived on little more than grit and recycled nitrogen. As {given_name} swallows the last of the ancestral sorghum's ghost, how will the family's authority be carried into the next cycle?

options.text: We temper the harsh iron of our station with new blood. I will bind our family to the hydroponic reclamation directorate through marriage, producing heirs who will inherit both our political cunning and their green-dome wealth.

The station cannot afford the luxury of domestic alliances. I will raise my sister's orphaned child as my sole successor, preserving the existing bond of our blood without bringing an outsider into the private chambers of {given_name}.

Let the line burn out like a dying thruster, so long as it burns bright. I will pour every ounce of our political capital into dismantling the Earth-bound trade quotas, leaving {surname} not as a family tree, but as the spark that freed the domes.

prose: The damp rot of green hemlock stumps and the sour, iron-rusted reek of waterlogged silt rose from the drainage cuts like cold gas. It was a sodden, deep-basin stink that never truly lifted from this northern valley, a world away from the sun-baked, clean dust of the Pearl River delta. Here, the earth smelled of ancient cedar needles decaying into black acid beneath the shovel, overlaid with the sharp, yellow grease of salted lard sizzling over green alder fires—a bitter wood-tar smoke that nested in the throat and made the ditch-crews squint through red-rimmed eyes.

Drifting beneath the hemlock was the damp, collective musk of the enclave’s survival: the alkaline tang of coarse grey cotton shirts, soaked in months of heavy ditch-sweat and dried over smoldering peat until they smelled of vinegar and old mutton-fat. There was no fragrant pork or star anise in these pots; instead, the steam from the shared iron cauldron carried the thin, briny stench of salt-cod and fermented mustard greens, the meager winter rations of men who slept three to a shelf in the District Association’s drafty timber hall.

Yet, through the clinging grey vapor of the camp, a drier, older scent held the ground—the clean, resinous drift of sandalwood incense burning in a chipped teacup before a paper image of Guan Di, tacked to a rough-split cedar post. It mingled with the sharp vinegar wash used to scrub the floorboards of the mutual-aid cabin, a medicinal bite of order maintained against the mud, where decades accumulated one damp winter at a time while the enclave waited to see if the territory’s laws would grind them to dust or finally let them bring their families across the sea.

The wind from the lower cuts brought the heavy, sulfurous reek of coal-smoke and hot iron from the railhead down the valley, a greasy smudge on the sky that smelled of backbreaking wages and the bitter, grey dust of black powder blasts.

choice.text: Scythe back the brush and clear more soil to feed the enclave ourselves.

prose: From the threshold of the district head's canvas lean-to drifted the toasted, peppery scent of warm oolong tea, slicing through the raw pine rot, accompanied by the low, rapid murmurs of the elders calculating the merchant council's newest levy.

choice.text: Gather at the threshold to join the elders' council on the new tax.

prose: The bite of the wind-salted clay never truly left your skin, even here in the crowded alleyways of the ward. Your collarbones were perpetually raw, rubbed red by the stiff hemp collar of a third-hand tunic, while your palms bore the thick, yellowed horn earned from years of swinging iron picks against the Sierra granite. The cold of the Sacramento silt seemed to have settled into the very joints of your fingers, making them stiff and clumsy as you sorted the cheap, cracked tallow candles that lit your basement bunk.

In the cramped room behind the district association, the horsehair stuffing of {family_name}'s salvaged stool pricked through its split cotton cover, biting into your thighs as you leaned over the wash-tub. A sudden, sticky warmth broke the cold—the damp, grease-smeared hand of your youngest child reaching up to pull at your sleeve, her fingers smelling faintly of sesame oil and the salted duck eggs saved for the autumn festival. You reached out to steady her, your calloused palm catching on the rough, unplaned fir of the doorframe where the years of her growth were marked in shallow knife-notches.

Survival in the ward was measured in the grit beneath your fingernails and the damp heat of shared breath. The air inside the brick tenement was a heavy, living thing, thick with the oily steam of boiling bitter melon and the dry, powdery touch of incense ash drifting from the small altar in the corner. Every hand that pressed yours in the dim hallway—whether passing a loan from the district chest or sharing the weight of a water cask—was rough as split pine, a hard, common shield raised against the hostile world outside these few blocks.
Your palms burned against the dry, splintered cedar of the alley gate as you shoved it home, feeling the heavy iron bolt click into its rusted bracket.

choice.text: Spend the afternoon reinforcing the perimeter fences, ensuring the safety of the household.

prose: The clasp of the dyer’s hand was hot and slick with alum, his fingers locking around your wrist with a desperate, crushing pressure that sealed the watchman’s pact without a single word.

choice.text: Organize neighbors to pool resources for a shared community watch system.

decision.prompt: How will you best secure your household's immediate future against the uncertainties of this new land?

options.text: Invest in a sturdy, well-stocked pantry and tools for self-sufficiency.
Dedicate time to building stronger alliances with other families in the settlement.

prose: The canyon of the settlement spoke in a raw, iron rattle, a ceaseless metallic din that shivered the thin split-cedar walls of the hillside shanties. Down along the creek, the high-pressure boiler of the district company's laundry coughed its wet, rhythmic steam, punctuated by the shrieking bite of a timber-mill’s circular blade through green fir. Beneath this roaring engine of the white men's frontier ran the softer, dry scrape of horse-hair brushes against inkstones in the Huiguan office, and the sharp, musical ring of silver trade dollars dropped onto scrubbed pine tables—each dull clink a tiny, cold accounting of another year severed from the Pearl River.

Through the cedar slats of the district assembly hall, {given_name}’s voice rose above the general clamor, a clear, resonant reed carrying the heavy burden of their collective grievances. It was a voice honed on the salt-winds of the delta, falling easily into the rounded cadences of the Sze Yup dialect before sharpening into the hard, defensive English needed for the tax-collector's ledger. The crowd answered in a low, sea-swell murmur—the dry, gravelly coughs of men whose lungs were slowly turning to stone in the railway cuts, and the sudden, sharp crack of a pipe-stem struck against a bench-top to seal an oath of mutual aid.

In the margins of the assembly, the domestic rhythm of the bachelor ward persisted: the high, bird-like whistling of the few children born before the exclusionary tide, the hollow thud of a mule kicking its stall, and the wet, crackling hiss of pork fat rendered with salted turnip in iron woks behind the boarding house. This was the auditory fabric of their survival, a fragile scaffolding of noise raised against the vast, silent indifference of the timberline. Every shout, every sizzle of oil, and every dry scrape of a shovel against the cold silt was a collective breath—an assertion that they still held their ground where others had faded into the brush.
The dry, pistol-shot crack of a bull-whip echoed from the skid-road, followed by the hoarse, raw shouting of a teamster hauling fir timbers toward the water-chute.

choice.text: Focus on opportunities to supply raw materials or labor to the growing local industries.

prose: An elder’s voice, smooth as river-worn jade and twice as hard, drifted from the veranda of the merchant house, his measured cadences warning the assembly against the new municipal charter that threatened to strip their meager district of its water rights.

choice.text: Attend the community meeting to understand and potentially influence the proposed ordinance.

thread.crossing: The bitter, earthy steam of over-boiled green tea barely masked the raw sting of cheap whiskey when {given_name} met the bloodshot glare of the {family_name} patriarch across the damp counter of the store. Over the shrieking din of the circular saw, they exchanged a silent, tense nod, finalizing an illicit trade of smuggled ginseng for a handful of iron railway spikes needed to secure the wash-houses. It was a fragile, unspoken truce between two outcasts who knew that survival in this mud-choked camp depended on what the foreman never saw.

prose: Look close enough at the mud-slicked spine of the settlement and you could see thirty years of survival written in the tilt of the rafters. To the west, the white merchants' balloon-frame storefronts rose like fresh-shorn teeth, weeping yellow pine-pitch under coats of cheap linseed oil that caught the glare of the high basin sun. But on this side of the ditch, the ward crouched low, a graying patchwork of scrap-fir and tarred felt where five families shared a single stove and the wind rattled the paper windows of the Huiguan. Here, the eye lingered on the tiny, hand-planed shingles and the faded red paper strips pasted to lintels—prayers for peace and safety that the dry mountain winters had bleached to the color of salt pork.

Beyond the washhouse flumes, the tiny garden strips shimmered with a stubborn, brilliant green that seemed to defy the dry alkali crust of the flats. Slender bamboo stakes, salvaged from shipping crates and bound with split reeds, held up the heavy, warty bells of bitter melon and the pale, ruffled leaves of mustard greens, every leaf kept alive by grey water carried from the sluice-tails in pair after pair of heavy oak buckets. It was a visible, scraping labor, a miniature green world carved out of the dry waste, while just past the ditch-line the great, wild sweeps of the preemption claims lay fallow and choked with tumbleweed, held by men who only visited the valley to collect rent or sign deeds in the back rooms of the bank.

At the crossing where the gravel gave way to the raw yellow clay of the ward, the new township hall stood white and sharp as a bone, its fresh-planed siding blinding in the noon glare. Beside its clean stone steps, the weathered pine board of the district association hung from an iron hook, its carved characters—promising mutual aid to any traveler from the home counties—worn so shallow by the wind that they were nearly flat. Below it, the road itself told the whole story: a dry, clean line of crushed river rock that stopped dead where the white shops ended, leaving the mud-holes of the Chinese quarter to swallow the wheels of the vegetable carts whole.

A sharp glint of sun caught the polished brass rim of the new civic marker in the square, its heavy cast plate engraving the town's 'founding' families in deep, angular letters that left no room for the strokes of a {surname} name.

choice.text: Note the glaring omission and consider ways to assert the community's contributions.

prose: Through the shimmer of the noon heat, you watched the county road-crew dump load after load of white mountain gravel into a new bypass, a clean loop designed to steer the heavy freight wagons wide of the ward's cabbage stalls and straight into the private yards of the dry-goods syndicate.

choice.text: See the road as a deliberate attempt to marginalize, prompting thoughts of direct action or protest.

decision.prompt: How will {given_name} {surname}'s generation shape the visibility and standing of your community in this developing town?

options.text: Launch a public campaign demanding formal representation and recognition of your community's contributions.
Focus resources on establishing a strong, independent economic hub within your community, proving self-sufficiency.
Forge a strategic alliance with a sympathetic, influential figure from the established town leadership, seeking incremental gains.

prose: The salt-boiled bitterness of wild dandelion greens, dug from the gravel-choked drainage ditches where the western Pacific railway spur ran out of steam, clung to the back of the throat like cold stove-soot. It was a lean, stringy forage, boiled down with nothing but a thumb-tip of lard and the sour memory of vinegar, yet it tasted of stubborn survival in a bachelor's ward that smelled always of wet coal and cabbage. Sitting across the grease-scarred fir table from {full_name}, whose heavy-lidded gaze watched the dwindling candle with the patience of an heir waiting for a decayed beam to split, the bite of those greens felt less like supper and more like a hard sermon chewed in common—a reminder that everything their mutual-aid association had built here remained rooted in the shifting silt of the white man's town.

Underneath the grease of the salt-pork lingered the dry, alkaline sting of the district office, and with it, the sour, metallic scrape of cheap iron-gall ink. It was a flavor that lived in the yellowed cracks under the fingernails and along the seam of the dry lip from wetting the steel nib through freezing midnights spent drafting protests for the laundrymen and tenants. To {full_name}, this vinegar-sharp ink-tongue was the only inheritance worth holding: not the rock-cluttered acres that barely met the county tax, but the literate teeth of the petition, the knowledge of which English clauses could freeze a constable's boot at the threshold or make a federal marshal squint twice at a resident's paper.

The jasmine tea that followed was thin, brewed from thrice-stretched dregs that had lost their perfume to the damp of the basement storehouse, leaving only the hot, tannic bite of well-water in a cup mended with lead wire. Yet as the steam rose between them, smelling faintly of camphor crates and parched earth, it offered a thin glaze of decency over the raw tallying of the ward’s upcoming precinct vote. This was the drink of men who had learned to buy their peace with two hands on the cup, hiding the split skin of the shovel-hand while the tongue calculated the exact cost of an alderman's blind eye.

The first draft from the new community pipe-line tasted not of the yellow river-silt or the lead of old solder, but of a cold, deep limestone clarity that had never belonged to their people until twenty shovel-crews broke their backs through the shale to claim it.

choice.text: Relish the practical improvements brought by collective effort, emphasizing sustainability.

prose: The raw corn-whiskey they poured to seal the compromise burned like kerosene, leaving a charred, medicinal heat in the mouth that promised nothing but a thumping temple at dawn and a temporary, borrowed stool at the back of the municipal hall.

choice.text: Savor the taste of hard-won political success, reinforcing the importance of continued activism.

decision.prompt: The cold steam of the dandelion broth rises between us, smelling of alkaline dust and the open, muddy ditches of the expansion zone. How will the {family_name} name endure when the gravel of this border town is all we have left to claim?

options.text: We must swallow our pride and bind {given_name} to the wealthy salt-merchant's daughter; let her dowry buy us the fertile land we need to raise three strong heirs who will never know the taste of weeds.

Avoid the complications of foreign blood. We will adopt your cousin's quiet boy from the salt-pans, teaching him to hoard our meager political secrets and carry the {surname} banner without the burden of a new dowry.
Pour every scrap of our remaining health and silver into securing {given_name} the clerkship at the district office; let the bloodline thin and fade if it means we die holding the seals of office.
